text:
The fix is not actually in place — `ring_buffer.js` still has the overflow bug, and the test that pins head/tail after overflow will fail.

Reading the `push` method: on overflow (`size === cap`) it advances head with `head = (head + 2) % cap`. That step is wrong — it should be `+ 1`. The inline `// wrong step` comment marks exactly this. When the buffer is full, the write lands at `(head + size) % cap === head`, overwriting the oldest slot correctly, but head must then move forward by exactly one to point at the new oldest element. Moving by two skips a live element, so both the logical order and the pinned head/tail values come out wrong.

Concretely, with `cap = 3` and pushes `1,2,3,4`, `toArray()` returns `[3, 4, 2]` instead of the expected `[2, 3, 4]`, and head ends at 2 rather than 1. The non-overflow path (`size < cap`) and `toArray()` itself are correct; the defect is isolated to the single overflow branch.

The one-line correction is to change `(head + 2) % cap` to `(head + 1) % cap`. I have not applied it, since this turn was a read-only review of the produced file — say the word and I'll make that edit.
